teamObjective.explanation: With no trumps and only low spades, leading the weakest card invites partner to take control with a higher card while preserving my limited high cards for later.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner has 3 unknown cards. Since no cards have been played, partner is roughly equally likely to hold higher spades, trump-rank cards, or other suits. I believe partner can plausibly beat a low spade lead but should not assume it.

opponentBelief.summary: Each opponent holds 3 unknown cards. They likely contain cards higher than spade-3, and possibly trump-rank 7s or red trumps. They will probably overtake a low spade lead if they can, but they cannot reveal hidden cards yet.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the spade-3 is the minimal commitment: it is the lowest card in my hand, least likely to win outright, and keeps the 4/5/6 for future tricks or to avoid being forced to play them under pressure.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Playing the 6 wastes my highest non-trump and still loses to almost any trump or higher card; it gives opponents more information without meaningful gain.
The 4 is only marginally stronger than the 3 but loses the same card-positioning advantage; keeping sequential low cards is more valuable.
The 5 sits in the middle of my hand and is better retained for a later lead or response than thrown away opening the trick.

risks: An opponent may play a trump or higher spade and keep the lead, forcing my team onto defense.
If partner is also weak, the opening 3 may simply pass control to opponents without learning much.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the lowest card I minimize resource loss regardless of outcome, leaving my slightly higher spades available for later tricks or to cover partner.
